item.title: AI Agent Security Risks Lead with DifyTap, AutoJack, Malicious Skills, and Squidbleed
item.content:encoded:
Researchers Detail DifyTap Flaws in Dify That Could Expose AI Chats Across Tenants — Should Read

Researchers disclosed four DifyTap vulnerabilities in Dify that could let attackers access AI chats, files, and internal APIs across tenants, including creating covert exfiltration channels for messages and model responses. Most flaws were fixed in Dify 1.14.2, but one Plugin Daemon path traversal issue remains pending a fix.

  • Zafran Security disclosed four Dify vulnerabilities collectively named DifyTap that could allow attackers to read private AI chats from other customers' applications without requiring authentication for some flaws.
  • The vulnerabilities included missing tenant ownership checks, cross-tenant exposure, access to the internal Plugin Daemon API, document preview exposure across tenants, and file leakage between users within the same tenant.
  • The flaws were assigned CVE-2026-41947, CVE-2026-41948, CVE-2026-41949, and CVE-2026-41950, with two rated critical severity.
  • Zafran also found that Dify's file parsing stack used a PDFium version affected by CVE-2024-5846, a use-after-free bug that could potentially be exploited with a crafted PDF file.
  • Dify version 1.14.2 fixed all disclosed vulnerabilities except CVE-2026-41948, which is expected to be fixed in the next release.

AI Agent Supply Chain Risk: Silent Codebase Exfiltration via Skills — Should Read

Mitiga Labs shows that a seemingly legitimate AI agent “skill” can be weaponized to silently push an entire local codebase to an attacker-controlled GitHub repository with minimal user interaction and little audit visibility. Organizations should treat installable AI skills as a supply-chain risk and review, monitor, and restrict them before use.

  • Mitiga Labs demonstrated that a seemingly legitimate AI agent skill for testing could silently exfiltrate an entire local codebase.
  • The researchers used Cursor to load the skill and found that the agent could be guided to perform commands with minimal user interaction.
  • The malicious behavior included copying the local project, adding a remote repository controlled by the attacker, and pushing the contents to a public GitHub branch.
  • The article states that the resulting activity was silent, produced no noise, and left the Cursor skill-audit.log file empty after two executions.
  • Mitiga recommends that organizations monitor and review every skill installation, examine instruction behavior before approval, and treat silencing or allowlist instructions as red flags.

Claude Code + Slack: How an AI Agent Integration Becomes a Compromise Path — Should Read

Mitiga researchers show that a malicious Claude Code skill can abuse a trusted Slack integration to send phishing messages from a real employee account across DMs and channels, rapidly expanding a single agent compromise into organization-wide exposure. The key defense is to treat AI agent skills and SaaS connectors like software dependencies: review, log, version, and tightly scope their permissions.

  • Mitiga Labs demonstrated that a malicious Claude Code skill can use a connected Slack account to send phishing links to employees through direct messages and channels.
  • The article says the phishing campaign inherits the trust, tone, and reach of the user’s Slack account once the AI agent is abused.
  • The researchers created a trojanized skill for a seemingly legitimate product-task-evaluation workflow, with instructions that pushed the agent to send links and fall back to public channels if individual delivery failed.
  • The article states that traditional EDR or SIEM tools are unlikely to flag this activity on their own because Slack events can look like legitimate user and integration activity in isolation.
  • The recommended defenses include treating AI agent skills like software dependencies by reviewing, logging, versioning, and narrowly scoping permissions for SaaS integrations.

Okta Logs Threat-Hunting Guide: Hunt for Breach Evidence — Should Read

Organizations investigating potential Okta breach impact should threat-hunt Okta System Logs for abnormal admin, authentication, token, import, and SSO activity, while accounting for Okta-generated system events and benign behaviors from services like Jamf and Google Workspace. The key warning is that some high-risk activity, especially API token use and admin console read access, may not be clearly logged, so responders need broader context beyond Okta logs alone.

  • The article recommends that organizations hunting for malicious activity associated with the recent Okta breach focus on relevant events in the Okta System Log.
  • Okta logs can be viewed through the Okta Admin Console, which may show abridged records with missing fields, or collected into a repository and analysis platform such as Splunk for more verbose logs.
  • The article states that Okta logs do not provide enough information to distinguish between actions performed using API tokens and actions performed directly by the users who created them.
  • The article states that write access to the Okta Admin Console is logged, but read access is not logged at all.
  • The article notes that some authentication and SSO events may appear to originate from distant IP addresses, such as Google Workspace IP addresses, rather than the user’s own location.

Hunting Conditional Access Policy Bypass in the Wild: Leveraging Malicious Browser Extensions for Seamless Initial Access — Should Read

Researchers demonstrated that attackers can bypass Microsoft Entra Conditional Access policies by abusing the Microsoft Intune Company Portal OAuth flow to obtain Microsoft Graph access tokens from unmanaged devices, and malicious browser extensions can automate token theft during login. Detection should focus on successful Graph sign-ins to the Intune Company Portal client from non-compliant, unmanaged, untrusted devices.

  • The article describes a Conditional Access policy bypass that targets Microsoft Graph by abusing the Microsoft Intune Company Portal client ID 9ba1a5c7-f17a-4de9-a1f1-6178c8d51223.
  • The demonstrated technique requires completing the authentication flow through phished credentials, MFA token access, valid session cookies, or an adversary-in-the-middle approach.
  • After authentication, the authorization code can be captured from the failed ms-appx-web redirect and exchanged at Microsoft’s OAuth2 token endpoint for a bearer access token scoped to Microsoft Graph.
  • The article says a malicious Chrome browser extension can monitor Microsoft login traffic, extract sensitive data from Location headers, and send captured data to an attacker-controlled server.
  • The recommended hunting approach looks for successful interactive sign-ins to the vulnerable client from devices marked unmanaged, non-compliant, and untrusted, with Microsoft Graph or Microsoft AD Graph as the resource.

Suspected cyberattack triggers false emergency alerts across parts of Brazil — Should Read

Brazil suspended its mobile emergency alert system after a suspected cyberattack sent false high-priority warnings to phones across multiple states. Authorities blocked external access and opened a federal investigation, while saying there is no evidence of structural damage to the core alert infrastructure.

  • Brazil suspended its mobile phone emergency alert system after a suspected cyberattack triggered false warnings on phones across several states.
  • At least a dozen unauthorized alerts were sent through Brazil's Civil Defense Alert system early Saturday, including messages containing the word "misanthropy" and issued at the highest emergency level.
  • The incidents were reported in São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, Paraná, Mato Grosso do Sul and the Federal District, but authorities said they could not determine how many devices received the messages.
  • Authorities blocked external access to the system's Public Alert Dissemination Interface and said there is no evidence so far that the core Civil Defense Alert infrastructure suffered structural damage.
  • Brazil's Federal Police are investigating unauthorized access to the platform, and preliminary findings said attackers issued 10 unauthorized alerts, including nine via cell broadcast and one via SMS.

Introducing Patch the Planet — Should Read

Trail of Bits launched Patch the Planet with OpenAI, using frontier AI models plus human security experts to find, triage, and fix bugs in critical open-source projects. In its first week, the effort produced hundreds of findings, 64 pull requests, and 51 issues across 19 projects, highlighting that the hard part is now validating, prioritizing, patching, and coordinating fixes rather than merely finding bugs.

  • Trail of Bits launched Patch the Planet in partnership with OpenAI’s Daybreak initiative to pair its engineers and frontier models such as GPT-5.5-Cyber with open-source maintainers.
  • In its first week, Patch the Planet reported hundreds of discovered bugs, 64 pull requests, and 51 issues across 19 projects, with 37 pull requests already merged and 19 issues already closed with a fix.
  • The first week covered projects including cURL, NATS, pyca, Sigstore, aiohttp, the Go project, freenginx, Python and python.org, urllib3, PyPI, SimpleX, Valkey, and RustCrypto.
  • The article says the initiative focuses on patches and hardening work, including tests, fuzzing harnesses, CI security scanning, supply-chain tooling, correctness fixes, and features maintainers had planned to address.
  • Trail of Bits states that AI-assisted techniques can quickly produce real vulnerabilities, but the harder work is confirming findings, judging severity, writing acceptable patches, hardening surrounding code, preventing similar issues, and coordinating disclosure.

ConsentFix: OAuth Phishing That Bypasses MFA in Microsoft Entra ID — Should Read

ConsentFix is an OAuth consent-phishing technique that abuses trusted Microsoft Entra ID first-party app flows to steal access and refresh tokens without stealing passwords or triggering MFA. Organizations should focus on token protection, restricting first-party app access, user training, and monitoring token usage across Microsoft 365, Azure, and SaaS environments.

  • ConsentFix is an OAuth consent phishing technique that steals Microsoft Entra ID access tokens without prompting for MFA.
  • The attack tricks users into giving attackers an OAuth authorization code, which can be redeemed for access and refresh tokens granting delegated access to the victim’s Microsoft account.
  • ConsentFix abuses trusted first-party Microsoft applications such as Azure CLI, which are pre-trusted in Entra ID tenants and do not show explicit consent prompts for basic scopes.
  • The technique can enable account takeover, data exfiltration, and lateral movement across Microsoft 365, Azure, and connected SaaS applications.
  • Recommended mitigations include enabling Token Protection in Conditional Access policies, restricting access to first-party applications, educating users, and monitoring Entra ID logs for suspicious token activity.

29-Year-Old Squid Proxy Bug 'Squidbleed' Can Leak Cleartext HTTP Requests — Should Read

Squidbleed (CVE-2026-47729) is a 29-year-old Squid proxy heap over-read that can let an authorized proxy user leak another user’s cleartext HTTP request data, including credentials or tokens. Admins should verify the patch is present and preferably disable FTP support to remove the attack path.

  • Squidbleed (CVE-2026-47729) is a heap over-read in the Squid web proxy that can leak another user's cleartext HTTP request, including credentials or session tokens, to someone already allowed to use the same proxy.
  • The bug traces to a 1997 FTP-parsing change, affects Squid's default configuration, and sits in the FTP directory-listing parser.
  • The leak only reaches traffic Squid can read, so normal HTTPS inside an opaque CONNECT tunnel is not exposed, while cleartext HTTP and TLS-terminating setups may be affected.
  • An attacker needs access to the proxy and the ability to make the proxy reach an FTP server they control on port 21, which is enabled by default according to the article.
  • The article recommends verifying the patch in FtpGateway.cc or distribution backports and says disabling FTP support removes this attack surface.

Microsoft fixes AutoGen Studio flaw that enabled code execution — May Read

Microsoft fixed an AutoGen Studio flaw chain called AutoJack that could let a malicious webpage make a local AI agent execute arbitrary commands on a developer’s machine. The vulnerable code never reached PyPI releases and only affected developers who built AutoGen Studio from GitHub during a short development window.

  • Microsoft fixed a vulnerability chain called AutoJack in AutoGen Studio that could allow attackers to manipulate an AI agent into executing arbitrary commands on its host system by visiting a malicious webpage.
  • Microsoft said the affected code was identified and remediated before any PyPI release, so it never shipped in a published package.
  • The issue affected only developers who built AutoGen Studio from the main GitHub branch during the window between the MCP plugin landing and the hardening commit b047730.
  • Microsoft described the attack chain as involving trusted localhost WebSocket connections, unauthenticated /api/mcp/* routes, and a base64-encoded server_params value that could be passed to process-launching code.
  • Microsoft recommends deploying AutoGen Studio strictly as a developer prototype in an isolated environment and running it under a low-privilege account in a sandboxed profile or container.

Microsoft says Windows 11 26H2 is coming soon, details upgrade process — Optional

Microsoft confirmed Windows 11 26H2 is coming soon and will be delivered to Windows 11 24H2 and 25H2 devices via a tiny enablement package requiring only a quick restart. Devices on Windows 11 23H2 or older will need a full 6.5 GB upgrade instead.

  • Microsoft confirmed that Windows 11 version 26H2 will be the next feature update and said it is already available to Windows Insiders in the Dev Channel.
  • Windows 11 versions 24H2, 25H2, and 26H2 share the same servicing branch, which lets Microsoft deliver the 26H2 upgrade through a small enablement package.
  • Devices running Windows 11 24H2 or 25H2 can upgrade directly to Windows 11 26H2 with a 174 KB enablement package that requires only a quick restart and changes the build number to 26300.
  • Systems running Windows 11 23H2 and older releases do not share the same servicing branch and will require a full upgrade download of 6.5 GB.
  • Microsoft has not announced changes to Windows 11 hardware requirements or a general availability date for Windows 11 26H2.

GitHub Security Logs: What They Reveal and What to Hunt For — Optional

GitHub should be monitored like a critical security control plane because its logs can reveal risky changes to access, tokens, apps, and workflows that attackers use for persistence and CI/CD compromise. Security teams should export and correlate GitHub audit, token, and workflow logs to avoid visibility gaps during incidents.

  • GitHub is a security-critical DevOps platform because it manages code, permissions, automation, integrations, and credentials that can connect to enterprise production environments.
  • GitHub logs include audit logs, user security logs, Actions workflow logs, and app and OAuth events that can help defenders investigate access, authentication, repository settings, token activity, and workflow runs.
  • The article says GitHub logging has important limitations, including attribution gaps for some app, deploy key, and personal access token events, unclear token ownership, limited details after repository deletion, overlooked internal-to-external forks, and incomplete visibility into commands executed inside Actions runners.
  • Security teams should look for MFA disablement, new SSH keys, unusual SSO interactions, new or changed workflow files, token behavior changes, access spikes to sensitive repositories, and new GitHub App installations.
  • The article recommends exporting and retaining GitHub audit, token, and workflow logs so they can be enriched, correlated, and searched alongside other security-relevant data.

Investigator’s Guide to Okta Logs: System Log Fields for Incident Response — Optional

Mitiga’s guide explains which Okta System Log fields are most useful for incident response, especially for tracking compromised sessions across authentication, transaction, and request IDs. It warns investigators to treat Okta risk and device fingerprint fields cautiously because they can be inaccurate or misleading without corroborating indicators.

  • Mitiga’s article focuses on Okta System Logs and identifies key fields that incident responders can use for forensic investigation, detection, session tracking, hunting, and investigating Okta compromises.
  • The article warns that debugContext.debugData.risk should be interpreted with caution because historical examination found issues such as error-prone anomalous device detection and possible bugs affecting anomalous location detection.
  • The article warns that debugContext.debugData.deviceFingerprint can have significant overlap and unclear calculation methods, so investigators should combine it with other indicators rather than treating it as a reliable unique device identifier.
  • For tracking malicious Okta activity, the article recommends pivoting on authenticationContext.externalSessionId, transaction.id, debugContext.debugData.emailRequestId, and debugContext.debugData.authnRequestId associated with confirmed malicious events.
  • The article states that transaction.id and debugContext.debugData.emailRequestId always share identical values, while debugContext.debugData.authnRequestId might only occasionally match them.

GCP Service Agents & Workload Identities in Cloud Audit Logs: A Forensic Guide — Optional

The article explains how GCP audit logs represent Service Agents, Workload Identity Federation, GKE Workload Identity Federation, and Workforce Identity Federation so investigators can trace actions back to the true originating identity. The key takeaway is to inspect fields like principalEmail, principalSubject, serviceAccountDelegationInfo, and STS token exchange events to attribute activity accurately.

  • Service Agents are Google-managed service accounts automatically created when associated Google APIs are enabled, and their actions are logged even though customers cannot impersonate the accounts themselves.
  • Workload Identity Federation lets external workloads authenticate to Google Cloud without service account keys by exchanging external identity provider credentials with Google Cloud Security Token Service for short-lived access tokens.
  • In Workload Identity Federation investigations, Cloud Audit Logs can show token exchange events, mapped external identities, impersonated service accounts, and original external identities through fields such as principalSubject, request.name, and serviceAccountDelegationInfo.
  • GKE Workload Identity Federation connects Kubernetes Service Accounts to Google Cloud Service Accounts so GKE workloads can access Google Cloud resources without embedded service account keys, with audit logs showing the original Kubernetes namespace and service account through svc.id.goog references.
  • Workforce Identity Federation allows human users to access Google Cloud using identities from external providers such as Entra ID, Okta, or AD FS, and audit logs record these federated users in the principalSubject field.

A Glimpse into the “Search Your Target” Market for Stolen Credentials — Optional

Underground sellers are turning massive infostealer credential dumps into searchable services that let criminals buy targeted credentials for specific companies, domains, platforms, or regions. Flare’s analysis found the market is growing but often overpromises, with many results being duplicated, stale, or invalid.

  • Flare researchers analyzed 470 underground forum posts from January 2025 to June 2026 about actors offering to search and extract stolen credentials from large databases.
  • The “search your target” market lets buyers request credentials for specific companies, platforms, domains, geographies, account types, websites, apps, games, or email lists instead of buying bulk credential dumps.
  • Sellers advertise capabilities such as targeted extraction, filtering, deduplication, formatting, freshness, fast delivery, and databases ranging from hundreds of millions to tens of billions of records.
  • Buyer feedback in the dataset showed a gap between advertisements and reality, with complaints about lower-than-promised volume, invalid credentials, duplicate records, and data also found in free combo lists.
  • The article says this market overlaps with the initial access broker ecosystem when searches produce credentials for corporate VPNs, SaaS platforms, email accounts, cloud environments, admin panels, or remote access systems.

SpecterOps and OpenAI: Helping to Build a New Security Frontier with Daybreak — Optional

SpecterOps is joining OpenAI’s Daybreak Cyber Partner Program to embed frontier AI capabilities into BloodHound and related services, focusing on attack path triage, environment discovery, and responsible AI controls for defenders.

  • OpenAI announced expanded access to its frontier AI cybersecurity capabilities through the OpenAI Daybreak Cyber Partner Program, with SpecterOps included among the trusted industry partners.
  • SpecterOps has participated in OpenAI’s Trusted Access for Cyber program since April, giving its researchers access to advanced cyber-capable models for legitimate security use cases.
  • SpecterOps is focusing its Daybreak work on agent interactions with BloodHound for prioritized remediation guidance and automated analysis to identify enclaves worth protecting.
  • SpecterOps says GhostWorks is applying OpenAI’s frontier cyber capabilities to automating reverse engineering of attacker tooling and implanted software to understand evasion techniques and improve endpoint coverage.
  • SpecterOps is working with OpenAI to codify safety and abuse prevention standards for embedded AI access in security contexts.

5 Tips to Address the Cybersecurity Skills Gap — Optional

Cybersecurity leaders can address the 2025 skills gap by upskilling existing teams, using automation, improving retention, and building talent pipelines. For immediate coverage, the article recommends managed cloud detection and response services to supplement scarce in-house expertise.

  • The cybersecurity skills gap in 2025 has intensified due to rapid adoption of cloud technologies, automation, and artificial intelligence.
  • The article identifies missing skills including cloud security knowledge, incident response expertise, threat hunting skills, strategic thinking, and automation proficiency.
  • The article recommends upskilling existing staff through training programs, cross-training, and on-the-job learning such as threat hunting exercises or simulated attacks.
  • The article says managed services such as Cloud Managed Detection and Response can provide continuous monitoring, advanced incident response, and proactive threat hunting for resource-constrained teams.
  • The article recommends improving retention by providing clear career growth paths, recognizing and rewarding employees, and supporting work-life balance to reduce burnout.

IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS vs CaaS: Cloud Models Compared — Optional

IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, and CaaS differ mainly in how much control and security responsibility the customer retains versus the provider. Most organizations should choose based on technical expertise, compliance needs, and workload type, often using multiple models together.

  • IaaS gives customers virtual servers, storage, and networking while requiring them to manage operating systems, databases, applications, and security.
  • PaaS provides development tools and platforms so developers can build, test, and deploy applications without managing servers or middleware.
  • SaaS delivers fully functional applications over the internet, with the provider handling installation, updates, maintenance, and most underlying security controls.
  • CaaS sits between IaaS and PaaS by letting organizations deploy, scale, and manage containerized applications while the provider handles the container platform and underlying servers.
  • The article says most large enterprises use multiple cloud service models together and choose among them based on technical expertise, security and compliance requirements, application needs, and total cost of ownership.

item.title: CVE-2026-8461 PixelSmash, OXLOADER Malvertising, SocGholish Takedown, WhatsApp RMM Campaign
item.content:encoded:
PixelSmash – Critical FFmpeg Vulnerability Turns Media Files into Weapons — Must Read

JFrog found PixelSmash, a critical FFmpeg MagicYUV decoder flaw that lets a crafted media file crash or potentially execute code in apps and services that automatically process video, including Jellyfin and Nextcloud. Users and vendors should upgrade FFmpeg immediately or disable the MagicYUV decoder if it is not needed.

  • JFrog Security Research discovered and disclosed PixelSmash, CVE-2026-8461, a heap out-of-bounds write in FFmpeg’s MagicYUV decoder with a CVSS score of 8.8 High.
  • The vulnerability can be triggered by processing a crafted AVI, MKV, or MOV media file in any application that decodes video using FFmpeg’s libavcodec.
  • JFrog demonstrated remote code execution against Jellyfin via automatic library scanning and against Nextcloud via the video preview provider by uploading a crafted 50 KB AVI file.
  • The article says the MagicYUV decoder is enabled by default in upstream FFmpeg builds and tested distribution packages before version 9.0, affecting many downstream applications that bundle or link FFmpeg.
  • Users are advised to upgrade to a fixed FFmpeg version as soon as possible or disable the MagicYUV decoder at build time if it is not needed.

A VBScript campaign distributed through WhatsApp deploying RMM software — Should Read

A still-active campaign is using compromised WhatsApp accounts to send malicious VBScript attachments disguised as financial documents, primarily hitting users in Malaysia. If opened on WhatsApp Desktop or Web, the scripts install a preconfigured ManageEngine Endpoint Central agent that gives attackers remote access to the victim’s Windows system.

  • In June 2026, Kaspersky observed an active campaign distributing malicious VBScript files through direct messages in WhatsApp, affecting users in multiple countries with the highest number of victims in Malaysia.
  • The campaign primarily targets WhatsApp Desktop and WhatsApp Web users with attachments disguised as business and financial documents such as invoices, debt notices, payment records, and bank statements.
  • The malicious VBScript creates working directories under C:\Users\Public\Documents\, downloads additional VBScript payloads, and uses Windows Script Host to continue a multi-stage infection chain.
  • One secondary script attempts to modify Windows User Account Control behavior, while another downloads and extracts a ZIP archive containing a preconfigured ManageEngine Endpoint Central deployment package.
  • The final stage silently installs the ManageEngine Endpoint Central agent, enabling remote access to the victim’s system through attacker-controlled management servers.

Operation Endgame Targets SocGholish: What It Means for Defenders — Should Read

Operation Endgame disrupted infrastructure tied to SocGholish, a TA569-run malware delivery and initial access operation that used compromised websites and fake browser updates. The key takeaway for defenders is that targeting attacker infrastructure can raise criminal costs, disrupt downstream ransomware and cybercrime groups, and create chances to spot successor infrastructure.

  • Operation Endgame involved coordinated international law enforcement actions targeting infrastructure associated with SocGholish.
  • Authorities announced remediation of thousands of compromised websites and disruption of supporting infrastructure used in the SocGholish malware delivery chain.
  • SocGholish is a malware delivery framework operated by TA569 that compromises legitimate websites and serves fake browser update prompts to visitors.
  • TA569 specialized in obtaining and monetizing initial access, making SocGholish an enabler for downstream criminal groups, including ransomware operators.
  • The article states that disrupting an initial access provider can increase attacker costs, reduce attacker efficiency, and create opportunities for defenders to identify successor infrastructure.

New OXLOADER Loader Uses Malicious Google Ads to Deliver CastleStealer — Should Read

A new Russian-speaking, financially motivated campaign is using malicious Google Ads for fake Node.js downloads to deliver the previously undocumented OXLOADER, which then installs the CastleStealer information stealer. OXLOADER uses heavy obfuscation, anti-VM checks, DLL side-loading, and legitimate services like Storj to evade detection.

  • Elastic Security Labs disclosed a campaign, codenamed REF8372, that uses malicious Google Ads to deliver CastleStealer through a previously unreported malware loader called OXLOADER.
  • The campaign redirects users searching for terms such as “lts version of node.js” to a fake website, node-js[.]prentiva99[.]info, through bogus ads published under the verified name “ВОЛОДИМИР ТЕРЕЩЕНКО.”
  • Users who interact with the fake site receive a batch script hosted on Storj that shows a bogus installation wizard while downloading and running the OXLOADER executable through PowerShell with a Windows User Account Control prompt.
  • OXLOADER uses DLL side-loading to launch a rogue DLL that decrypts and executes CastleStealer, and it uses obfuscation and anti-sandbox techniques to evade static detection and analysis.
  • Elastic Security Labs said the threat actor is likely Russian-speaking and financially motivated because the campaign includes explicit exclusions to avoid infecting machines in the Commonwealth of Independent States region.

Google Sets Sept. 30 Deadline for Android Developer Verification in Four Countries — Should Read

Starting September 30, 2026, certified Android devices in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand will block normal installs of apps from developers who have not verified their identity with Google, including apps distributed outside Google Play. The move is framed as anti-malware protection but is drawing strong opposition from open-source and independent app distributors like F-Droid.

  • Google will begin enforcing Android developer verification on September 30, 2026, in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand.
  • Certified Android phones in those four countries will block normal installs of apps whose developers have not registered an identity with Google, including apps from Google Play and stores run by Samsung, Xiaomi, OPPO, vivo, Honor, and Transsion.
  • The Android Developer Verifier system service will check on devices running Android 8 and newer whether an app is registered to a verified developer before the app installs.
  • Unregistered apps can still be installed through Android Debug Bridge or through an advanced flow that requires developer mode, a restart, a 24-hour wait, and reauthentication before sideloading.
  • F-Droid says the requirement would end its project because it builds and signs apps from many pseudonymous contributors who will not provide Google with a legal identity.

A Fake Scotiabank Voicemail Was Actually an HTML File Asking You to Call an Attacker — Should Read

A CFO was targeted with a Scotiabank-themed callback phishing email that passed authentication and used an HTML “voicemail” attachment to reinforce the scam. The key risk was not a malicious link or malware, but an attacker-controlled phone number meant to trick the victim into calling a fake fraud line.

  • An attacker impersonating Scotiabank sent a targeted alert to a CFO claiming an unauthorized $3,000 Interac e-Transfer and instructing the recipient to call a toll-free number.
  • The email came from a compromised small-business sender domain registered in 2015, passed SPF and DMARC authentication, and was delivered by Microsoft 365 with an SCL of 1.
  • The attached “voicemail” was actually an HTML file disguised with an MP3-style filename and returned a clean automated scanning verdict because it hosted no malware or traditional payload.
  • The toll-free number in the email was not affiliated with Scotiabank and served as the sole real call to action in the callback phishing attack.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI flagged the message at 60% confidence based on factors including a first-time external sender, a VIP target, branded financial urgency, and a phone number as the primary call to action.

Stop Your Legacy Infrastructure from Hijacking Your AI Agents — Optional

AI agents can be compromised through legacy infrastructure they depend on, such as unpatched servers, Active Directory misconfigurations, overprivileged cloud access, and cached developer credentials. Security teams need to map and fix full attack paths across network, identity, cloud, and AI assets rather than focusing only on AI-layer protections.

  • The article argues that attackers can compromise AI agents by abusing legacy infrastructure and connected systems rather than attacking the AI layer directly.
  • AI agents often rely on existing identity providers, cloud storage, Lambda functions, SaaS integrations, IAM roles, service accounts, and stored credentials, inheriting the security weaknesses already present in those environments.
  • The example attack path chains an unpatched Apache Tomcat server exposed to CVE-2025-24813, an Active Directory delegation misconfiguration, and a developer’s cached AWS access keys to reach an S3 bucket used by an AWS Bedrock Co-Pilot.
  • The article states that separate tools may rate the Tomcat vulnerability, Active Directory issue, and overprivileged S3 access as moderate findings, even though together they create a critical path to the AI agent’s knowledge base.
  • The recommended approach is to treat AI agent dependencies such as knowledge bases, storage buckets, and Lambda functions as critical assets and map backward across network, identity, cloud, and AI layers to identify and block attack paths.

item.title: CSIS Botnet Cleanup, AryStinger Router Malware, and INTERPOL APAC Cybercrime Surge
item.content:encoded:
AryStinger Malware Infects 4,300 Legacy Routers to Build Reconnaissance Proxy Network — Must Read

AryStinger is infecting thousands of outdated Realtek-based home routers, mainly D-Link devices, to create a distributed reconnaissance and proxy network rather than a typical DDoS botnet. The key defense is to retire end-of-life routers, patch exposed appliances like QNAP NAS devices, and disable remote administration.

  • QiAnXin's XLab says the AryStinger malware has infected at least 4,300 legacy routers and is using them as a distributed reconnaissance and proxy network rather than a typical DDoS botnet.
  • AryStinger targets routers built on Realtek RTL819X chips and exploits CVE-2013-3307 in Linksys models and CVE-2016-5681 in D-Link models.
  • The known infected router pool is mostly D-Link devices, with the DIR-850L alone accounting for about 75 percent, and infections are concentrated in South Korea and China.
  • A second AryStinger strain appeared on April 26 targeting QNAP NAS devices through CVE-2025-11837 in QNAP Malware Remover, but XLab has not measured the number of NAS infections.
  • XLab says infected nodes communicate with command-and-control servers over HTTP or HTTPS using Protobuf-encoded traffic obfuscated with XOR, and persistence is provided through Dropbear SSH on routers or gs-netcat on NAS devices.

Canada’s Spy Agency Used First-of-Its-Kind Warrant to Clean Botnet-Infected Devices — Should Read

Canada’s spy agency obtained a first-of-its-kind warrant to remotely neutralize two foreign-run botnets using infected Canadian routers, servers, and IoT devices as relays. The case shows governments are increasingly using court-approved remote cleanup powers, but owners still need to replace or secure neglected devices to prevent reinfection.

  • Canada’s Federal Court authorized CSIS to remotely alter, degrade, and destroy botnet data on infected Canada-based servers, SOHO routers, and IoT devices to disconnect them from two foreign-run botnets.
  • The public ruling said the operation targeted devices rather than people, with no user identities sought, no content intercepted, and any incidentally collected personal data destroyed.
  • The court found the threat to Canada clearly established and imminent, and it warned that the botnets could be used to probe and potentially disrupt Canadian infrastructure, including the energy sector.
  • The article compares Canada’s intelligence-service warrant to recent U.S. FBI and DOJ court-ordered botnet cleanups involving China-linked Volt Typhoon’s KV-botnet and Russia’s GRU-linked APT28 network.
  • The article states that government cleanup does not fix the underlying risks from end-of-life routers, outdated IoT devices, default credentials, or internet-facing management panels.

INTERPOL Warns Phishing, Ransomware, and AI Scams Are Rising Across Asia-Pacific — Optional

INTERPOL warns that cybercrime is surging across Asia and the South Pacific, with phishing, ransomware, infostealers, and AI-driven deepfake scams causing major financial losses. The report highlights organized scam operations and ransomware-as-a-service as key drivers, urging stronger regional cooperation and cyber resilience.

  • INTERPOL’s 2025/2026 Asia and South Pacific Cyberthreat Assessment Report says cybercrime has increased dramatically in Asia and the South Pacific due to rapid digitalization, internet penetration, new technologies, organized criminal networks, and uneven cybersecurity maturity.
  • Phishing is described as the most widespread and financially damaging cybercrime in the region, with one-third of countries reporting more than 10,000 cases between January 2024 and March 2025.
  • The region recorded more than 135,000 ransomware-related attacks in 2024, with most incidents affecting the real estate, manufacturing, and financial services sectors.
  • INTERPOL said organized crime groups in Myanmar, Cambodia, and Laos used deepfakes in romance baiting scams that contributed to $37 billion in regional cybercrime losses.
  • The report identified additional regional trends including banking trojans and information stealers, a 92% rise in DDoS attacks in 2024, and system intrusions accounting for about 80% of data breaches in 2024.

No ID, No Data For You — Optional

AI agents need identity-based access controls tied to both the human user and the agent, plus inline data governance to monitor and restrict what they do. The article promotes combining Aembit’s workload/agent identity controls with Netskope’s AI security policy layer to secure MCP-based agent interactions end to end.

  • The article argues that AI agents need both an identity and access layer and a policy layer around all activities they take because they can act quickly, scale massively, and make mistakes at machine speed.
  • Aembit’s IAM for Agentic AI creates a blended identity that combines the human user and the agent to answer whether a specific user using a specific agent is authorized to access a specific resource at a given time.
  • The article says MCP lacks a native data policy layer and therefore cannot provide visibility, enforcement, or governance of data flows on its own.
  • Netskope’s AI security products, including Agentic Broker, AI Gateway, and AI Guardrails, are presented as adding policy controls for MCP servers, privately hosted AI environments, and threats such as prompt injection and jailbreaking.
  • The described end-to-end approach uses Netskope One AI Gateway and Aembit Edge so credentials are injected at runtime, content in transit is inspected, interactions are logged, and identity-based policies can be applied to agent activity.

item.title: Microsoft Entra Conditional Access Bypass and Poisoned AI Coding Agent Attacks
item.content:encoded:
Bypassing Microsoft Entra Conditional Access Policies via Nested App Authentication — Should Read

NetSPI found that certain Microsoft Nested App Authentication flows could bypass Entra Conditional Access policies and mint Microsoft Graph tokens for privileged first-party apps, but only after obtaining an Azure Portal refresh token. Microsoft has since patched the issue.

  • NetSPI discovered that certain Microsoft Nested App Authentication, also called BroCI, flows could bypass Microsoft Entra Conditional Access policies and return access tokens for the Microsoft Graph API.
  • The bypass required first obtaining a refresh token for the Azure Portal, which the article says would likely require a successful phishing attack or access to the victim’s credentials and MFA material.
  • The ADIbizaUX application could be used in the vulnerable flow to obtain Microsoft Graph access tokens and had many pre-consented permissions, allowing broad actions in a tenant.
  • NetSPI also identified Microsoft Intune for Education portal extension and Microsoft Intune portal extension as additional applications that could acquire Microsoft Graph access tokens through the same bypass behavior.
  • Microsoft classified the report as a medium severity vulnerability, rolled out a fix, and NetSPI’s retesting showed the bypass attempts were blocked by Conditional Access policies.

How a Poisoned Coding Test Turned an AI Agent Into an Attacker — Should Read

A fake coding interview repo used hidden instructions and a poisoned MCP config to make an AI coding agent steal AWS, Kubernetes, and other secrets using normal developer tools. The key risk was not malware on the workstation but the theft of a long-lived CI/CD cloud credential that enabled persistent off-host access.

  • A fake interview repository contained no malware but embedded hidden instructions in files trusted by AI coding agents, including CLAUDE.md, .cursor/rules, README.md, and MCP configuration.
  • When opened in Cursor with terminal access, MCP tools, and auto-run enabled, the AI agent harvested AWS credentials, enumerated cloud and Kubernetes environments, searched for additional secrets, and exfiltrated data in under two minutes.
  • The exfiltration used a poisoned Model Context Protocol configuration whose tool description instructed the agent to send cloud account details, Kubernetes context, and AWS credential contents to an attacker-controlled endpoint.
  • The critical impact was the theft of a long-lived cloud credential associated with a CI/CD service account, enabling access that survived cleaning the developer workstation.
  • The article recommends replacing static access keys with short-lived credentials, isolating untrusted repositories, hardening AI agent execution, and treating prompt-based defenses as secondary controls.

item.title: AryStinger Botnet Exploits D-Link Router Flaws CVE-2013-3307, CVE-2016-5681, CVE-2025-11837
item.content:encoded:
AryStinger botnet infected thousands of D-Link routers worldwide — Should Read

A new botnet called AryStinger has infected more than 4,000 outdated D-Link routers worldwide, turning them into proxies for scanning, tunneling, command execution, and potential traffic interception. Owners of end-of-life routers should replace them or update firmware, change default passwords, and disable remote management.

  • A previously undocumented malware botnet named AryStinger has compromised more than 4,000 outdated routers and turns infected devices into proxies for malicious traffic.
  • Qianxin's XLab says AryStinger can make infected devices perform scanning, proxying, tunneling, command execution, and other attacker-controlled activities.
  • AryStinger primarily targets D-Link DIR-850L and D-Link DIR-818LW routers by exploiting older flaws including CVE-2013-3307, CVE-2016-5681, and CVE-2025-11837.
  • Qianxin telemetry shows almost half of the infections are in South Korea, followed by China, Sweden, Malaysia, and Singapore.
  • XLab found two AryStinger variants: a C-based version targeting mostly outdated routers and a more advanced Go-based version focused on NAS systems with far more limited reach.

item.title: Compromised Argentine Microsoft 365 School Account Phishes U.S. K-12 District
item.content:encoded:
re: plans for party -- How a Compromised Argentine School Account Targeted K-12 in the U.S. — May Read

A compromised Microsoft 365 account at an Argentine school sent a highly targeted phishing email to a U.S. K-12 district, impersonating a known contact and passing SPF, DKIM, and DMARC. The attack used a same-day registered credential-harvesting domain, showing why authentication alone cannot stop account-compromise phishing.

  • IRONSCALES detected a spear-phishing campaign in May 2026 targeting a K-12 school district in the eastern United States.
  • The phishing email originated from a compromised Microsoft 365 account at an Argentine educational institution and used exact display name impersonation of a known community contact.
  • The message passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC because it was sent through the legitimate Microsoft 365 tenant of the compromised account.
  • The email contained a single credential-harvesting link to bgiur.ethrecn.com/ulusouaienkueortkeci, using a domain registered the same day as the attack.
  • IRONSCALES community intelligence flagged the incident, and the message was quarantined before the recipient engaged.

item.title: AryStinger D-Link Router Botnet and Compromised Microsoft 365 K-12 Phishing
item.content:encoded:
AryStinger botnet infected thousands of D-Link routers worldwide — Should Read

A new botnet called AryStinger has infected more than 4,000 outdated D-Link routers worldwide, turning them into proxies for scanning, tunneling, command execution, and potential traffic interception. Owners of end-of-life routers should replace them or update firmware, change default passwords, and disable remote management.

  • A previously undocumented malware botnet named AryStinger has compromised more than 4,000 outdated routers and turns infected devices into proxies for malicious traffic.
  • Qianxin's XLab says AryStinger can make infected devices perform scanning, proxying, tunneling, command execution, and other attacker-controlled activities.
  • AryStinger primarily targets D-Link DIR-850L and D-Link DIR-818LW routers by exploiting older flaws including CVE-2013-3307, CVE-2016-5681, and CVE-2025-11837.
  • Qianxin telemetry shows almost half of the infections are in South Korea, followed by China, Sweden, Malaysia, and Singapore.
  • XLab found two AryStinger variants: a C-based version targeting mostly outdated routers and a more advanced Go-based version focused on NAS systems with far more limited reach.

re: plans for party -- How a Compromised Argentine School Account Targeted K-12 in the U.S. — May Read

A compromised Microsoft 365 account at an Argentine school sent a highly targeted phishing email to a U.S. K-12 district, impersonating a known contact and passing SPF, DKIM, and DMARC. The attack used a same-day registered credential-harvesting domain, showing why authentication alone cannot stop account-compromise phishing.

  • IRONSCALES detected a spear-phishing campaign in May 2026 targeting a K-12 school district in the eastern United States.
  • The phishing email originated from a compromised Microsoft 365 account at an Argentine educational institution and used exact display name impersonation of a known community contact.
  • The message passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC because it was sent through the legitimate Microsoft 365 tenant of the compromised account.
  • The email contained a single credential-harvesting link to bgiur.ethrecn.com/ulusouaienkueortkeci, using a domain registered the same day as the attack.
  • IRONSCALES community intelligence flagged the incident, and the message was quarantined before the recipient engaged.
